methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Quantum Brilliance is a Series A quantum computing company founded by Mark Luo that has raised $50M+ to date. The quantum computing sector comprises 3 publicly traded peers (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private companies including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion, though a narrow peer set at identical stage and valuation tier is limited.

The Stage-Progression method is the applicable triangulation approach, using the $50M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x multiplier typical for Series A progression to subsequent stages, yielding $100M. The Peer-Median method is not applicable due to insufficient narrow comparables—while 7 same-sector peers exist, fewer than 3 datapoints met filters for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier classification, making sector-wide medians unreliable for this exercise.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $85M (low) to $115M (high), with a median point estimate of $100M. This range reflects reliance on a single methodology; the primary uncertainty is the absence of direct comparable transaction multiples at this stage and maturity in quantum computing. Further narrowing would require additional private company financials or market data within the quantum computing Series A cohort.
